questions.question: Let S be the set of all ternary strings defined over the alphabet {a, b, c}. Consider all strings in S that contain at least one occurrence of two consecutive symbols, that is, 'aa', 'bb', or 'cc'. The number of such strings of length 5 that are possible is __________. (Answer in integer)
questions.to_find: Number of ternary strings of length 5 containing at least one occurrence of 'aa', 'bb', or 'cc'

understand.plain: We count ternary strings of length 5 over {a, b, c} that have at least one pair of adjacent identical characters. The cleanest approach is complementary counting: subtract from the total the strings that have NO two adjacent characters equal. A string with no consecutive repeat means each character differs from its immediate predecessor.

keywords.explain: A sequence of characters where each position is chosen from a 3-symbol alphabet {a, b, c}.
keywords.example: 'aabca' is a ternary string of length 5 containing the consecutive pair 'aa' at positions 1-2.

keywords.explain: Count desired outcomes by subtracting unwanted outcomes from the total: |wanted| = |total| - |unwanted|.
keywords.example: Strings with >= 1 repeat pair = (all strings) - (strings with no repeat pair).

keywords.explain: A string where every adjacent pair of characters is distinct.
keywords.example: 'abcab' has no two adjacent identical symbols; 'abacb' also qualifies.

understand.visual_alt: A ternary string of length 5 with boxes for each position showing that adjacent positions must differ in the complement set.

given.aim: Count length-5 ternary strings with at least one consecutive identical-symbol pair using complementary counting

terms.meaning: 3-symbol set; each position has 3 independent choices
terms.connects: Gives the universe size for complementary counting.

terms.meaning: The string has 5 positions and 4 adjacent pairs to check
terms.example: Positions: [P1, P2, P3, P4, P5]; pairs: (P1,P2), (P2,P3), (P3,P4), (P4,P5).
terms.connects: Determines how many choices the complement-count formula uses.

terms.meaning: Strings where every character differs from the one immediately before it
terms.example: P1 has 3 choices; each of P2, P3, P4, P5 has 2 choices (anything but the previous).

given.plan: Use complementary counting: (strings with >= 1 repeat pair) = 3^5 - (strings with no repeat pair). For no-repeat strings: position 1 has 3 choices, each subsequent position has exactly 2 choices.

steps.title: Count total ternary strings of length 5
steps.apply: Each of 5 positions chooses freely from {a,b,c}: Total = 3^5 = 243
steps.note: All strings, with or without consecutive repeats.

steps.title: Count strings with NO consecutive identical symbols
steps.formula_raw: n1 x n2 x ... x nm
steps.apply: Position 1: 3 choices. Each of positions 2, 3, 4, 5: 2 choices (any symbol != previous). No-repeat count = 3 x 2 x 2 x 2 x 2 = 3 x 2^4 = 48
steps.note: Each new position is forbidden only the single symbol of its predecessor.

steps.title: Apply complementary counting
steps.apply: Strings with at least one consecutive repeat = 243 - 48 = 195
steps.note: This counts every string that has 'aa', 'bb', or 'cc' anywhere in it.

questions.formula_note: Complementary counting subtracts the 3 x 2^4 = 48 no-repeat strings from the 3^5 = 243 total, giving 195.

questions.question:
A line L in a circuit is said to have a stuck-at-0 fault if L permanently has a logic value 0. Similarly, L is said to have a stuck-at-1 fault if L permanently has a logic value 1. A circuit is said to have a multiple fault if one or more lines have stuck-at faults. The total number of distinct multiple faults possible in a circuit with N lines is:
A. 3^N - 1
B. 3^(N-1)
C. 2^N - 1
D. 2
questions.to_find: Total number of distinct multiple faults in a circuit with N lines

understand.plain: Each line in an N-line circuit can be in exactly one of three independent states: normal (no fault), stuck-at-0, or stuck-at-1. A 'multiple fault' is any combination where at least one line has a stuck-at fault. By the product rule, there are 3^N total state combinations. The only excluded case is where every line is normal, so the answer is 3^N - 1.

keywords.explain: A line that is permanently fixed at logic 0 (stuck-at-0) or logic 1 (stuck-at-1).
keywords.example: In a 2-line circuit, one fault scenario: line 1 stuck-at-0, line 2 normal.

keywords.explain: Any fault condition where at least one line has a stuck-at fault (even a single faulted line qualifies).
keywords.example: A circuit with only line 3 stuck-at-1 is a valid multiple fault scenario.

understand.visual_alt: N lines in a circuit each independently in one of three states (normal, stuck-at-0, stuck-at-1), with all-normal excluded.

given.aim: Count all combinations of line states across N lines that include at least one stuck-at fault

terms.example: For N=1: 3 states total, minus 1 (normal) = 2 faults possible.
terms.connects: Product rule gives 3^N total combinations across N lines.

terms.meaning: The circuit has N independently faultable lines
terms.example: For N=2: 3^2 = 9 combinations minus 1 all-normal = 8 distinct multiple faults.

terms.meaning: The unique case where every line is in the normal (no-fault) state
terms.example: This is 1 case out of 3^N — it is excluded since it has no stuck-at faults.

given.plan: Each of N lines independently has 3 states. By the product rule, total state combinations = 3^N. Subtract 1 (the all-normal case) to count only multiple faults.

steps.title: Apply the product rule across all N lines
steps.formula_raw: n1 x n2 x ... x nm
steps.apply: Each of N lines has 3 states: {normal, stuck-at-0, stuck-at-1}. Total combinations = 3 x 3 x ... (N times) = 3^N
steps.note: Lines are independent, so the product rule applies directly.

steps.title: Subtract the all-normal (no-fault) case
steps.apply: The one combination where all N lines are normal is excluded. Distinct multiple faults = 3^N - 1
steps.note: A 'multiple fault' requires at least one line to be stuck-at, so the no-fault state does not count.

questions.formula_note: Product rule gives 3^N total line-state combinations; subtracting 1 removes the all-normal (no fault) case, leaving 3^N - 1.

questions.question:
In how many ways can n blue balls and m red balls be distributed in k distinct boxes such that each box contains at least one ball of each colour?
A. C(n-1, k-1) * C(m-1, k-1)
B. (n+m)! / ((n-1)! * (m-1)!)
C. n! / (k! * (n-k)!) * m! / (k! * (m-k)!)
D. C(n+m-1, k-1)
questions.to_find: Number of ways to distribute n identical blue balls and m identical red balls into k distinct boxes with at least one ball of each colour per box

understand.plain: The blue and red balls are identical within each colour, and the boxes are distinct (labelled). The two colours are distributed independently. For each colour, we must place identical balls into k distinct boxes so that each box gets at least one. By stars-and-bars with a minimum-one constraint, distributing n identical balls into k labelled boxes with each box >= 1 gives C(n-1, k-1) ways. The same holds for the red balls: C(m-1, k-1). Since the two distributions are independent, we multiply.

keywords.explain: A technique to count ways to distribute r identical objects into n distinct bins. With each bin >= 1, it gives C(r-1, n-1).
keywords.example: Distributing 5 identical blue balls into 3 boxes (each >= 1): substitute y_i = x_i - 1, get C(4,2)=6 ways.

keywords.explain: Reserve one object for each box first, then distribute the remainder freely.
keywords.example: n balls into k boxes with each box >= 1: set y_i = x_i - 1 >= 0, then y_1+...+y_k = n-k, solutions = C(n-1, k-1).

understand.visual_alt: n identical blue balls and m identical red balls distributed separately into k labelled boxes, each box receiving at least one of each colour.

given.aim: Count ways to distribute n identical blue and m identical red balls into k distinct boxes with >= 1 of each colour per box

terms.term: n identical blue balls into k distinct boxes (each >= 1)
terms.meaning: Stars-and-bars with minimum constraint: C(n-1, k-1) ways
terms.example: For n=4, k=2: C(3,1)=3 splits (1+3, 2+2, 3+1 across the 2 boxes).
terms.connects: Provides the first factor in the product.

terms.term: m identical red balls into k distinct boxes (each >= 1)
terms.meaning: Stars-and-bars with minimum constraint: C(m-1, k-1) ways
terms.connects: Provides the second factor in the product.

terms.meaning: The blue distribution and the red distribution are chosen independently
terms.example: Choosing how the blue balls are placed does not affect how the red balls are placed.
terms.connects: The product rule lets us multiply the two counts.

given.plan: Split into two independent sub-problems. Blue: C(n-1, k-1). Red: C(m-1, k-1). Multiply by the product rule for the total.

steps.title: Distribute n identical blue balls into k boxes, each box >= 1
steps.formula_raw: x_1 + x_2 + ... + x_k = n => C(n-1, k-1) solutions with each x_i >= 1
steps.apply: Substitute y_i = x_i - 1 (so y_i >= 0): y_1 + ... + y_k = n - k. Non-negative solutions = C((n-k)+(k-1), k-1) = C(n-1, k-1)
steps.note: This is the standard stars-and-bars formula with a minimum-one-per-box constraint.

steps.title: Distribute m identical red balls into k boxes, each box >= 1
steps.formula_raw: x_1 + x_2 + ... + x_k = m => C(m-1, k-1) solutions with each x_i >= 1
steps.apply: By the same argument, ways = C(m-1, k-1)
steps.note: The red balls are distributed independently of the blue balls.

steps.title: Combine using the product rule
steps.formula_raw: n1 x n2 x ... x nm
steps.apply: Total ways = C(n-1, k-1) x C(m-1, k-1)
steps.note: Blue and red distributions are independent, so we multiply the two counts.

questions.formula_note: Stars-and-bars with minimum-one-per-box gives C(n-1,k-1) for blue and C(m-1,k-1) for red; independence gives the product.

questions.question:
The number of substrings (of all lengths inclusive) that can be formed from a character string of length n is:
A. n
B. n^2
C. n(n+1)/2
D. 2^n
questions.to_find: Total number of substrings of all possible lengths from a string of length n

understand.plain: A substring is a contiguous block of characters defined by a starting position and a length. For a string of length n, substrings of length k can start at positions 1 through n-k+1, giving (n-k+1) such substrings. Summing over all lengths from 1 to n yields n + (n-1) + ... + 1, which is the triangular number n(n+1)/2.

keywords.explain: A contiguous sequence of characters within the original string, defined by a start index and a length.
keywords.example: From 'abcd', substrings of length 2 are 'ab' (start 1), 'bc' (start 2), 'cd' (start 3) — that is 3 = 4-2+1 substrings.

keywords.explain: The sum 1 + 2 + ... + n equals n(n+1)/2, known as the nth triangular number.

understand.visual_alt: A string of length n with brackets illustrating how substrings of each length k span n-k+1 starting positions.

given.aim: Count all substrings of lengths 1 through n of a character string of length n

terms.meaning: A sequence of n characters at positions 1 through n
terms.example: For n=3 ('abc'): all substrings are a, b, c, ab, bc, abc — total 6 = 3*4/2.
terms.connects: Defines the domain over which substrings are enumerated.

terms.meaning: Contiguous blocks of exactly k characters; can start at positions 1 through n-k+1
terms.example: For n=5, k=3: substrings start at positions 1,2,3 — giving 3 = 5-3+1 substrings.
terms.connects: There are (n-k+1) substrings of length k for each k from 1 to n.
This is the formula for the nth triangular number: n(n+1)/2.

given.plan: For each length k from 1 to n, count (n-k+1) substrings. Sum over k: sum_{k=1}^{n} (n-k+1) = n + (n-1) + ... + 1 = n(n+1)/2.

steps.title: Count substrings of a fixed length k
steps.formula_raw: n1 x n2 x ... x nm
steps.apply: A substring of length k starts at some position i where 1 <= i <= n-k+1. So there are (n - k + 1) substrings of length k.
steps.note: Starting positions beyond n-k+1 would let the substring exceed the string boundary.

steps.title: Sum over all possible lengths k = 1 to n
steps.note: Setting j = n-k+1 transforms the sum into 1+2+...+n, the standard triangular sum.

questions.formula_note: Substrings of length k have (n-k+1) starting positions; summing for k = 1 to n gives the triangular sum n(n+1)/2.

questions.question:
There are 6 jobs with distinct difficulty levels, and 3 computers with distinct processing speeds. Each job is assigned to a computer such that:
- The fastest computer gets the toughest job and the slowest computer gets the easiest job.
- Every computer gets at least one job.
The number of ways in which this can be done is ______.
A. 3
B. 5
C. 65
D. 15
questions.to_find: Number of valid assignments of 6 ordered jobs to 3 ordered computers given two fixed assignments and each computer getting at least one job

understand.plain: Label computers C1 (slowest), C2 (medium), C3 (fastest) and jobs J1 (easiest) through J6 (hardest). Two assignments are forced: J1 to C1 and J6 to C3. The 4 remaining jobs (J2, J3, J4, J5) must be assigned to the 3 computers so that C2 gets at least one job (C1 and C3 already each have one). The count is (all assignments of 4 jobs to 3 computers) minus (assignments where C2 gets nothing).

keywords.explain: The easiest job is locked to the slowest computer and the hardest job to the fastest computer by the problem constraints.
keywords.example: J1 -> C1 and J6 -> C3 are determined; only J2, J3, J4, J5 remain free.

keywords.explain: Every computer must receive at least one job; C1 and C3 already satisfy this via fixed assignments.
keywords.example: We only need to ensure C2 receives at least one of J2, J3, J4, J5.

keywords.explain: Count all assignments of the 4 free jobs to 3 computers, then subtract those where C2 gets none.
keywords.example: 3^4 total minus 2^4 where all free jobs go only to C1 or C3: 81 - 16 = 65.

understand.visual_alt: Three computers C1, C2, C3 with J1 fixed to C1 and J6 fixed to C3; jobs J2 through J5 are freely assigned subject to C2 getting at least one.

given.aim: Assign 6 jobs to 3 computers with two constraints fixed and every computer receiving at least one job

terms.meaning: J1 (easiest) -> C1 (slowest) and J6 (hardest) -> C3 (fastest) are fixed by the problem
terms.example: These do not contribute to the count — they are always present.
terms.connects: After fixing, we need to distribute J2, J3, J4, J5 to C1, C2, C3.

terms.meaning: Each of these 4 jobs can independently go to any of the 3 computers
terms.example: 3^4 = 81 total assignments without restrictions on C2.
terms.connects: We subtract the cases where C2 gets none.

terms.meaning: C1 and C3 already satisfy the at-least-one requirement via fixed jobs; only C2 might end up empty
terms.example: If all 4 free jobs go to C1 and C3 only: 2^4 = 16 bad assignments.

given.plan: Step 1: Fix J1 -> C1, J6 -> C3. Step 2: Each of J2, J3, J4, J5 goes to one of {C1, C2, C3}: 3^4 = 81 ways. Step 3: Subtract assignments where C2 gets no free job (all 4 go to C1 or C3): 2^4 = 16. Answer = 81 - 16 = 65.

steps.title: Fix the two constrained assignments
steps.formula_raw: n1 x n2 x ... x nm
steps.apply: J1 -> C1 (slowest) and J6 -> C3 (fastest): both are forced. Free jobs remaining: J2, J3, J4, J5.
steps.note: These two assignments are not choices — they reduce the problem to assigning 4 jobs.

steps.title: Count all unconstrained assignments of the 4 free jobs
steps.apply: Each of 4 free jobs goes to one of 3 computers independently: 3^4 = 81 ways

steps.title: Subtract assignments where C2 gets none of the free jobs
steps.apply: If C2 gets no free job, each of the 4 free jobs goes to C1 or C3 only: 2^4 = 16 bad assignments. Valid assignments = 81 - 16 = 65
steps.note: C1 already has J1 and C3 already has J6, so they satisfy the at-least-one constraint regardless. Only C2 needs to get a free job.

questions.formula_note: After fixing two extreme assignments, the 4 free jobs can go to any of 3 computers (3^4 = 81) minus the 2^4 = 16 cases where the middle computer gets nothing, giving 65.
